detail:
The claim's code logic is accurate and confirmed end-to-end. _stale_provisioning_tenants_queryset (services.py:89-97) deliberately includes Tenant.Status.ACTIVE in the repair candidate set, filtered to rows missing container_id OR container_fqdn. repair_stale_tenant_provisioning calls provision_tenant(tenant_id_str) for each candidate (services.py:154). But provision_tenant short-circuits for any status not in PENDING/PROVISIONING (services.py:212-220): it logs provision_skipped_unexpected_status and returns without doing work. The repair loop then re-reads the tenant (services.py:155-163); still ACTIVE with empty container fields, ready is False, so it is counted as incomplete and increments summary['failed']. An ACTIVE tenant with empty container metadata is therefore structurally un-repairable by this sweep and reports failed on every run, with no path that re-provisions it.

The intent-vs-behavior mismatch is real and internal to this code: the author explicitly placed ACTIVE in scope (a healthy ACTIVE tenant is never swept, since it has both container fields), signaling the sweep is meant to repair corrupted-ACTIVE rows, yet provision_tenant refuses to act on them. Not a misread, not guarded upstream, not handled elsewhere.

Reachability of the ACTIVE+empty state is low, which is why low severity is correct. Normal provisioning sets container_id/fqdn and status=ACTIVE in one atomic save (services.py:355-371), so it can't arise there; deprovision clears the fields but sets DELETED not ACTIVE (services.py:746-748). The one path that flips to ACTIVE without re-verifying container fields is reactivation: handle_checkout_completed flips SUSPENDED->ACTIVE on the assumption a hibernated container exists (billing/services.py:333-337). A suspended tenant normally retains its container fields, so reaching ACTIVE+empty requires prior corruption — but that is exactly the corruption class the repair sweep names itself to fix. No regression (this is original design), so it is a partial/incomplete-fix gap: the tool advertises (via ACTIVE-inclusion) repair of stale-ACTIVE tenants but cannot deliver it, leaving such a tenant's assistant unreachable until an operator manually intervenes.
suggested_fix: Make the repair path able to act on the ACTIVE-but-incomplete case. Two reasonable options: (1) In provision_tenant, broaden the status guard so that an ACTIVE tenant missing container_id/container_fqdn is treated as re-provisionable (e.g. allow ACTIVE when container fields are empty, resetting status to PROVISIONING before the create flow). (2) Alternatively, in repair_stale_tenant_provisioning, demote a matched ACTIVE+empty tenant to PROVISIONING (or PENDING) before calling provision_tenant, so the existing guard accepts it. Option 2 is the smaller, more contained change and keeps provision_tenant's invariant intact. Either way, add a test covering an ACTIVE tenant with empty container fields running through the sweep and ending repaired.
evidence: apps/orchestrator/services.py:89-97 — queryset includes Tenant.Status.ACTIVE alongside PENDING/PROVISIONING, filtered on container_id='' OR container_fqdn=''
apps/orchestrator/services.py:154-163 — repair calls provision_tenant then refresh_from_db; non-ready (still ACTIVE, empty fields) counts as incomplete -> summary['failed']
apps/orchestrator/services.py:212-220 — provision_tenant returns immediately for any status not PENDING/PROVISIONING, logging provision_skipped_unexpected_status
apps/orchestrator/services.py:355-371 — normal provisioning sets container_id/fqdn and status=ACTIVE atomically (healthy ACTIVE always has fields, never swept)
apps/orchestrator/services.py:746-748 — deprovision clears container fields but sets status=DELETED, not ACTIVE
apps/billing/services.py:333-337 — handle_checkout_completed flips SUSPENDED->ACTIVE without re-verifying container fields (the only realistic path to ACTIVE+empty, given prior corruption)
